title: Lemongrass Honey
text:
I want to be known for who I am,
And not boxed into who I could be.
I don’t want to feel like a program;
I want to be completely set free.
I want to be fully understood,
For what I think and what I do.
I want to be reassured,
That they understand my point of view.
I want to be deeply cherished
Like lemongrass and honey
Will you say this before I am perished?
Will I ever be somebody?
To be loved is to be known;
And to be known is to be loved.
Can you say that without hesitation?
Or do you have to be shoved?
I want to be lemongrass honey:
Sour with a kick of sweetness,
Head empty, sometimes funny,
Only then can I feel complete.

title: Girlnough
text:
I could wake up in the morning,
Wash my face and slap on lotion,
Patiently wait for it to dry,
While folding my cleaned laundry.
I could clip my hair with cute pins,
Sharpen my eyebrows with tweezers,
Trim my bangs just below my eyes,
Curl my hair into bouncy Js.
I could look almost effortless,
Use concealer as foundation,
Cherry pink under my eyebags,
Add eyeshadow as eyeliner.
I could wear off the shoulder tops,
Paired with high-waisted baggy jeans,
Chunky sneakers to finish it,
Now I almost look like a girl.
I could smile if they glance at me,
Ask them questions about their day,
Be interested in their life,
I almost blend in with the girls.
I suppose I could do much more,
In fact, I can’t help but compare,
How they are well groomed and pretty,
So why don’t I feel pretty too?
Their beauty and my beauty clash,
Different aesthetics and preference,
I’m trying to play on their home field,
It’s no wonder I feel awkward.
Maybe I don’t need to blend in,
Or find out what is wrong with me.
I don’t need their type of pretty,
I want mine to make me alive.

title: Mika Kohno
text:
Hello!
I write poems and occasionally post thoughts or small observations that don’t really fit anywhere.
This is a space for those fragments, ranging from writing, ideas, and whatever else inspires me over time. Or in other words: a place where poetry, thought, and learning coexist without hierarchy.



Snoopy from Peanuts by Charles M. Schulz

title: First-year in Second/Third-year Class
text:
One of the most common questions I get in class is: why are you here?
It’s a good question, considering it does seem bizarre how a first-year is taking a class primarily composed of second or third-year students. Usually, my answer depends on how much I feel like explaining. The short version is dual enrollment. That also explains my senior standing.
Even so, I feel out of place.
As a first-year microbiology major, I’m taking general microbiology with other micro majors who have been in college longer than I have. Most of the time, I don’t notice the difference. That is, until the conversation shifts. They talk about bar crawls, internships, and things that feel out of reach for me.
And then there’s studying. They seem to know how to do it. They know how to prepare for exams, how to manage their time, or how to get into research. It’s difficult to say if I even figured out what I should do. It took me until this third quarter just to start settling in.
It’s not just a social change, but it’s also a huge shift in academics. In high school and community college, I got away with barely studying. I would do some homework, a few practice problems, maybe pay attention in class, and that was enough. University isn’t as forgiving. Exams demand critical thinking and labs carry significant weight. It’s easy to get Bs but the gap between As and Bs feels much wider.
I also wouldn’t call myself a studious person. I enjoy learning, but I struggle staying focused in class and working through material methodically.
Despite all of this, being ahead in the curriculum has its benefits. The main one: pestering my upperclassmen with questions. After getting over the initial hesitation, it’s become something I enjoy.
Through them, I learned so much about research, my major, possible career paths, and which classes are worth taking. More than that, I’ve started to enjoy asking questions.
I’ve never been someone who asks questions easily. I’ve always had them, but saying them out loud used to feel awkward. I’m not sure what changed, but somewhere along the way, I started asking and it made everything a little clearer.
That’s the thing, isn’t it? I’m not learning at the same pace as everyone else. I’m learning how to ask questions.
